examples.title: BGP Route Selection Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm demonstrating BGP's best path selection process using path attributes
examples.explanation: BGP uses hierarchical path selection criteria. Routes are progressively filtered until one remains. LOCAL-PREF is the first and most important criterion, followed by AS-PATH length, then MED. Router ID is final tiebreaker.

examples.title: AS-PATH Loop Detection Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to detect routing loops by checking if own AS number exists in the AS-PATH
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckForLoop(asPath, myASNumber)
BEGIN
 // asPath is array of AS numbers in order
 // myASNumber is this router's AS

 FOR each asNumber IN asPath DO
 IF asNumber EQUALS myASNumber THEN
 // Loop detected - own AS found in path
 RETURN TRUE // Reject route
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR

 // No loop detected
 RETURN FALSE // Accept route
END

FUNCTION ProcessBGPRoute(routeUpdate, myASNumber)
BEGIN
 // Extract AS-PATH from route update
 asPath ← routeUpdate.AS_PATH

 // Check for loop
 IF CheckForLoop(asPath, myASNumber) THEN
 LOG "Route rejected: Loop detected - AS" + myASNumber + " in path"
 RETURN REJECT_ROUTE
 ENDIF

 // Prepend own AS before advertising
 newAsPath ← myASNumber + asPath
 routeUpdate.AS_PATH ← newAsPath

 // Advertise to other neighbors
 ADVERTISE_ROUTE(routeUpdate)
 RETURN ACCEPT_ROUTE
END
examples.explanation: Before accepting any BGP route update, the router checks if its own AS number already exists in the AS-PATH attribute. If found, this indicates a routing loop and the route is rejected. When propagating routes, the router prepends its own AS number to the AS-PATH.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k) where k = length of AS-PATH

examples.title: BGP Policy Configuration Example
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how to configure BGP routing policies using attributes
examples.code:
// BGP Policy Configuration Example

// Policy 1: Set LOCAL-PREF for outbound traffic selection
POLICY SetLocalPref
BEGIN
 IF neighbor IN customerList THEN
 SET localPref = 200 // Prefer customer paths
 ELSE IF neighbor IN providerList THEN
 SET localPref = 100 // Default preference
 ELSE IF neighbor IN peerList THEN
 SET localPref = 150 // Peers preferred over providers
 ENDIF
 APPLY_TO routes
END

// Policy 2: Filter routes based on AS-PATH
POLICY FilterASPath
BEGIN
 FOR each route IN incomingRoutes DO
 IF route.asPath MATCHES "^65001" THEN
 // Accept routes originating from AS65001
 ACCEPT route
 ELSE IF route.asPath MATCHES ".*65002.*" THEN
 // Reject if route passes through AS65002
 REJECT route
 ELSE
 ACCEPT route
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
END

// Policy 3: Set MED to influence inbound traffic
POLICY SetMED
BEGIN
 FOR each route IN outboundRoutes DO
 IF interface IS primaryLink THEN
 SET med = 100 // Primary link preferred
 ELSE IF interface IS backupLink THEN
 SET med = 200 // Backup link
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
END
examples.explanation: BGP policies allow network operators to control routing decisions beyond simple shortest-path selection. LOCAL-PREF controls outbound traffic paths, AS-PATH filtering provides security and policy enforcement, and MED influences how neighboring ASes send traffic to this AS.
